claim.hypothesis: Products predominantly associated with utilitarian attitudes at the category level support less utilitarian and more symbolic attitudes at the brand level. Products predominantly associated with symbolic attitudes at the category level support less symbolic and more utilitarian attitudes at the brand level.
claim.hypothesis_location: Section: The Effect of Branding on Attitude Functions; p. 350.
claim.statement: ...products supporting predominantly utilitarian category-level attitudes supported somewhat more symbolic attitudes when branded, whereas products supporting predominantly symbolic category-level attitudes supported less symbolic attitudes when branded…predicted level ×product type interaction (F(1, 204) = 7.70, p = .006).
claim.statement_location: Section: The Effect of Branding on Attitude Functions; Subsection: STUDY 1B: A BROADER SET OF CATEGORIES AND BRANDS; p. 351.

data.source: Random survey experiment run by authors

method.description: The authors run an RCT on students where they evaluate the utilitarian and symbolic aspects of products when they are branded or not branded and then analyze the results using mixed ANOVA analysis.
method.steps: (1) Run pilot study; (2) Choose the products, both utilitarian (paper towels, toothpaste, vitamins, and cough syrup) and symbolic products (college T-shirts, American flags, class rings, and greeting cards), and construct the experimental survey; (3) Recruit students and randomly assign them to evaluate branded and non-branded products; (4) run the experiment; (5) Analyze the results using two-by-two mixed ANOVA analysis.

results.summary: products supporting predominantly utilitarian category-level attitudes supported somewhat more symbolic attitudes when branded, whereas products supporting predominantly symbolic category-level attitudes supported less symbolic attitudes when branded

metadata.original_paper_title: Branding Alters Attitude Functions and Reduces the Advantage of Function-Matching Persuasive Appeals
